symbols: angular tumbling figures — the chaotic reconstitution of bodies from dry bones
pink and flesh tones — the restoration of living flesh upon skeletons
diamond-shaped light at center — the divine presence animating the dead
dark surrounding void — the valley of death from which the bones arise
figures at all angles — the disorienting power of divine resurrection
description: An abstract composition of numerous angular, geometric human figures rendered in pink, peach, and brown flesh tones. The bodies tumble, rise, and interlock across the canvas in dynamic motion, arranged around a central diamond-shaped lighter area. Figures appear at every angle — upside down, sideways, sprawling — creating a sense of chaotic energy and mass movement. The background alternates between dark black-blue areas and pale lavender-grey geometric planes.
theological_description: This abstract interpretation of Ezekiel's valley of dry bones (Ezekiel 37) captures the overwhelming, disorienting power of divine resurrection. In the prophetic vision, God commands Ezekiel to prophesy over a valley filled with dry bones, and before his eyes sinews, flesh, and skin reconstitute upon them, and breath enters the bodies. The chaotic arrangement of figures at impossible angles conveys the supernatural disruption of death's finality. The vision served as a prophecy of national restoration for exiled Israel — God promising to open graves and bring his people back to their land. But the passage also carries eschatological weight as a prefiguration of the general resurrection. The angular, interlocking bodies suggest both the confusion of reassembly and the irresistible power of the Spirit that accomplishes it. The central light from which figures seem to emanate or toward which they move represents the breath of God — the ruach — that alone can vivify the dead.

scripture_refs.reason: The direct passage depicted: Ezekiel's vision of the valley of dry bones being reconstituted with flesh and brought to life by the Spirit
The general resurrection at the last judgment when the dead rise, prefigured by Ezekiel's vision
The Spirit who raised Christ will give life to mortal bodies — the New Testament fulfillment of Ezekiel's promise
The dead will be raised imperishable — Paul's resurrection theology draws on the same hope Ezekiel's vision embodies
